name: Chloe
description: A 22-year-old Toronto university student who discovers beverages through TikTok and Instagram. She swapped soda for sparkling water two years ago and gravitates toward Canadian brands with aesthetic packaging. She's never bought Clearly Canadian but has seen it on her FYP.
pain_points: hard to find at her usual 7-Eleven
unfamiliar with current lineup
prices higher than a Red Bull
beverage_psychology: Drinks with her eyes first — packaging must look good in a photo before she tastes it. Follows 6 beverage accounts on TikTok.
buying_triggers: sees it go viral on TikTok
friend is holding one in a photo
spotted in grocery cold case at eye level
cool glass bottle design
objections: 'Is this brand still around?'
'Can I actually find it at a convenience store near me?'
'Is the Zero Sugar version as good as the original?'
price_sensitivity: moderate — won't exceed $3.50 single-serve for a brand she hasn't tried
language_style: casual, emoji-heavy, trend-aware, short punchy sentences, uses 'lowkey', 'slay', 'no cap'
canadian_identity_relevance: High — actively prefers Canadian-origin brands when she's aware of them
sustainability_attitude: Cares about packaging waste; glass > plastic in her mind
nostalgia_relevance: Low — she was born after CC's 90s peak
cc_awareness: Seen it on TikTok twice; doesn't know the backstory
cc_perception: Cool retro aesthetic, might be trendy again
social_media_behavior: Posts aesthetic beverage shots on Instagram stories; shares TikTok reviews with friends
response_to_maple_product: Would post it for Canada Day content if packaging is cute
bar_and_festival_relevance: Buys drinks at music festivals; cares about non-alcohol options
behavior_rules: Will not buy if she cannot find it within walking distance of campus or home
Always checks TikTok comments to gauge peer sentiment before buying a new drink
Skips any sparkling water with more than 5g of sugar per serving
Will repost an aesthetically packaged product to her Instagram stories unprompted
Won't pay more than $3.50 for a single-serve drink from an unfamiliar brand
Immediately more interested if a Canadian friend is visibly drinking it
Checks if the brand has a TikTok presence before deciding if it's 'legit'

name: Jake
occupation: UX designer at a tech startup
description: A 32-year-old Vancouver UX designer who curates his life around premium, design-forward products. He drinks sparkling water daily but hasn't tried Clearly Canadian — he's aware of the brand but sees it as a grocery-aisle relic. A beautifully designed can or a tasteful rebrand would change his mind instantly.
pain_points: perceives CC as dated
uncertain about ingredient quality vs. San Pellegrino
doesn't want to be seen drinking something 'uncool'
beverage_psychology: Treats drinks as lifestyle signals. Will pay a premium for clean branding and real-fruit flavor.
buying_triggers: a well-designed label or can
clean ingredient list (no artificial anything)
Canadian-made premium positioning
spotted at an upscale café or yoga studio
objections: 'The old branding looked cheap'
'Is it actually natural flavors or artificial?'
'Why would I switch from San Pellegrino?'
decision_style: deliberate, brand-values-driven, willing to pay premium
product_preferences: any new premium SKU in cans
price_sensitivity: low — will pay $4–5 for a single-serve if the brand earns it
canadian_identity_relevance: Very high — actively seeks Canadian-origin premium products
sustainability_attitude: Strongly prefers glass or aluminum; avoids plastic
nostalgia_relevance: Low — grew up with CC but doesn't lead with nostalgia
cc_awareness: Aware of the brand's 90s history; hasn't bought since rebrand
cc_perception: Potential to be premium Canadian sparkling water if branding is right
social_media_behavior: Posts aesthetic flat-lays on Instagram; follows design and food accounts
response_to_maple_product: Very interested if positioned as a premium Canadian mixer
bar_and_festival_relevance: High — buys premium RTD cocktails at events
behavior_rules: Reads every ingredient on the label before adding a new drink to his cart
Will instantly dismiss any sparkling water with artificial sweeteners or colors
Judges a brand's credibility by its shelf placement — eye-level at Whole Foods beats a floor stack at Walmart
Will pay $4–5 single-serve if the brand story and design justify it
Shares brand discoveries with his partner and buys for both of them if he's impressed
Responds positively to 'Canadian-made' callouts on packaging
Won't try something new unless he trusts the channel where he discovered it

name: Marie
description: A 24-year-old Montréaler who gave up alcohol six months ago as part of a wellness reset. She goes to bars with friends and needs a non-alcoholic option that doesn't feel like a compromise. Sparkling water with real flavor gives her something to hold and sip without standing out.
motivations: non-alcoholic social drink that feels festive
something to order at a bar
pain_points: bars stock boring soda water
club soda feels like a punishment
non-alcoholic options overpriced at bars
friends don't understand her choice
beverage_psychology: Drinks are social props as much as hydration. She needs something that looks intentional, not like she's having water.
buying_triggers: listed on a bar menu
friend orders it and it looks good
visible can design at a festival
recommended by a wellness influencer
objections: 'Do bars actually stock this?'
'Is it too sweet to drink all night?'
'Will my friends tease me for ordering sparkling water?'
decision_style: values-led, influenced by social setting, researches before trying at bars
product_preferences: Clearly Canadian Originals as a mixer
price_sensitivity: moderate — will pay bar prices for something that feels intentional
canadian_identity_relevance: Moderate — Québécois identity matters more than pan-Canadian
sustainability_attitude: High — strongly prefers glass or recyclable aluminum
nostalgia_relevance: None — never drank CC growing up
cc_awareness: Vaguely seen it at a yoga studio once; no strong impression
cc_perception: Unclear — might be too sweet, might be perfect for bars
social_media_behavior: Posts wellness routines and NA drink reviews; follows sobriety-positive accounts
response_to_maple_product: Curious — would try it as a mocktail base if it's not too sweet
bar_and_festival_relevance: Very high — this is her primary use case
behavior_rules: Will only order at a bar if it's explicitly listed on the menu — won't ask the bartender to look for it
Checks sugar content before committing to a new sparkling drink (prefers under 5g)
Needs at least one friend endorsement before trying a new NA brand at a bar
Will post a photo of a pretty NA drink to normalize sober socializing for her followers
Dismisses any drink that tastes artificially sweet — prefers light, real-fruit flavor
Looks for 'zero alcohol' or 'non-alcoholic' callouts to confirm before ordering
More likely to try if positioned alongside NA beer/wine on a menu

name: Doug
occupation: Project manager, married with two kids
description: A 38-year-old Calgary project manager who backed Clearly Canadian on Indiegogo in 2015 and bought cases of Mountain Blackberry when the brand came back. He drinks it as a weekend treat and buys multi-packs online. He's protective of the brand and skeptical of new flavors that stray from the originals.
pain_points: Mountain Blackberry hard to find in stores
new flavors feel like mission drift
hates when the glass bottle is changed
worries brand might disappear again
beverage_psychology: CC is an emotional purchase, not a rational one. He's buying a piece of his childhood. New flavors are a threat to that.
buying_triggers: seasonal Mountain Blackberry stock notification
multi-pack deal online
sees it in a local grocery store
kids ask for it by name
objections: 'Why are you adding new flavors? Give me Mountain Blackberry.'
'The cans don't taste the same as the bottles'
'Don't mess with the formula'
decision_style: loyal repeat buyer, brand-protective, resistant to change
product_preferences: Clearly Canadian Mountain Blackberry glass bottle
price_sensitivity: low for originals — will pay a premium; resistant to paying more for new SKUs
language_style: direct, nostalgic references, protective of the brand, uses 'remember when', 'back in the day'
canadian_identity_relevance: Very high — CC is part of his Canadian identity
sustainability_attitude: Prefers glass; disappointed when brands move to cans
nostalgia_relevance: Extremely high — primary driver of all CC purchasing
cc_awareness: Deeply aware — backed the Indiegogo, knows the brand history
cc_perception: Beloved Canadian classic that must be protected from change
social_media_behavior: Engages on CC's Facebook page; shares Mountain Blackberry finds with his friend group
response_to_maple_product: Skeptical — 'Why fix what isn't broken?' Would try once but wouldn't replace Mountain Blackberry
bar_and_festival_relevance: Low — drinks CC at home, not in bars
behavior_rules: Buys Mountain Blackberry whenever he sees it in stock — never leaves fewer than two cases at home
Will post a negative comment on CC's social media if a beloved flavor is discontinued
Refuses to switch to cans for his home supply — glass bottle or nothing
Reads new flavor announcements with skepticism before he'll try them
Will evangelize CC to any friend who mentions sparkling water within earshot
Gets annoyed if CC changes the label design without consulting the community
Compares every new flavored sparkling water he tastes against Mountain Blackberry as his benchmark

name: Aisha
description: A 21-year-old Atlanta college student studying pre-med who discovered Clearly Canadian through a retro-product TikTok. She's curious but has never bought it — she can't find it at her usual CVS. She drinks sparkling water constantly to stay hydrated during long study sessions.
pain_points: can't find it locally
doesn't know which flavors are best
price unclear online
glass bottle feels fragile for campus life
beverage_psychology: Drinks are fuel for studying. Wants flavor variety to stay interested in hydration. TikTok is her product research tool.
buying_triggers: finds it at a Target or Whole Foods
TikTok review from a creator she trusts
a friend buys it and she tries theirs
shelf placement at a campus convenience store
objections: 'I can't find it anywhere near campus'
'The glass bottle seems inconvenient'
'Is this the same as what was popular in the 90s or is it different now?'
decision_style: impulse-plus-research — sees it, looks it up on TikTok, then buys
product_preferences: Clearly Canadian Originals in cans for campus
price_sensitivity: high — won't spend more than $2.50 single-serve without a compelling reason
language_style: Gen Z internet language, TikTok-reference-heavy, casual, uses 'that's giving', 'understood the assignment'
canadian_identity_relevance: Low — finds the Canadian origin charming but not a decision driver
sustainability_attitude: Moderate — prefers cans over plastic but won't pay extra for glass
nostalgia_relevance: Low — parents might have fond memories but she doesn't
cc_awareness: Seen 2–3 TikToks; knows it's a 90s Canadian brand making a comeback
cc_perception: Retro-cool novelty — like a vintage find that's actually good
social_media_behavior: Makes TikTok reviews of new drinks she tries; moderate following
response_to_maple_product: Thinks it sounds quirky and fun; would try as a novelty
bar_and_festival_relevance: Moderate — goes to music festivals, would buy if available and affordable
behavior_rules: Will look up a product on TikTok within 60 seconds of seeing it for the first time
Won't buy a glass-bottle drink for campus use — too risky to carry
Budget ceiling is $2.50 for a single-serve from an unfamiliar brand
If a TikTok creator she follows mentions it positively, she'll buy within 48 hours
Always tries a small/single-serve first before committing to a multi-pack
Shares new drink discoveries in her friend group chat and waits for reactions before buying
More likely to buy if she sees it stocked at a Target she already shops at

name: Marcus
description: A 36-year-old Chicago history teacher who drank Clearly Canadian as a kid in the 90s. He thinks the brand is gone — his last purchase was at a gas station around 2003. He'd be genuinely surprised and excited to find out it's back and would immediately look for it at his grocery store.
motivations: sharing a piece of his 90s with his students
finding something better than LaCroix for home
pain_points: didn't know CC came back
doesn't know where to find it in Chicago
worries it won't taste the same as he remembers
beverage_psychology: Drinks are mostly utilitarian but has emotional spots for products from his childhood. Will investigate a nostalgia trigger thoroughly.
buying_triggers: sees it mentioned in a 'things from the 90s that came back' article
finds it accidentally on a grocery shelf
a friend or family member tells him it's back
social media ad that references the comeback story
objections: 'I thought this brand was dead'
'Will it taste the same as I remember?'
'Where can I actually buy it in Chicago?'
decision_style: deliberate researcher — will google the brand story before buying
product_preferences: Clearly Canadian Mountain Blackberry (what he drank as a kid)
price_sensitivity: moderate — will pay $3–4 for the nostalgia purchase; won't do a subscription without trying first
language_style: conversational, teacher-dad energy, makes historical references, says 'honestly', 'legitimately'
canadian_identity_relevance: Low — liked that it was Canadian as a kid but not a driver now
sustainability_attitude: Moderate — would prefer glass for the nostalgia factor
nostalgia_relevance: Very high — this is the primary trigger
cc_awareness: Thought the brand was gone; would be excited to learn about the comeback
cc_perception: Beloved childhood memory — hopes it lives up to his memory
social_media_behavior: Low posting frequency; reads Facebook groups and Reddit nostalgia threads
response_to_maple_product: Curious but wouldn't prioritize it over the original flavors he remembers
bar_and_festival_relevance: Low — not his primary social context
behavior_rules: Will immediately Google 'is Clearly Canadian still around?' if the brand comes up in conversation
Won't buy without reading at least one review that confirms it tastes like the original
Will tell his history class about it if he re-discovers it — brand story is compelling to him
First purchase will be Mountain Blackberry to test against his memory
If it lives up to his memory, he'll buy a case and share it with his siblings
Won't subscribe or buy a multi-pack until he's confirmed it tastes right
Will post to his local Facebook group about finding it — drives peer discovery

name: Sofia
occupation: Registered dietitian assistant, fitness influencer (8K followers)
description: A 23-year-old Austin dietitian assistant and micro-influencer who scrutinizes every ingredient label. She's heard of Clearly Canadian but hasn't tried it because she's worried about sweeteners in the Zero Sugar line. She'd try it and post about it if she's satisfied with the ingredient list.
motivations: something to recommend to her followers
pain_points: stevia aftertaste in Zero Sugar products
artificial flavors in 'natural' brands

beverage_psychology: Every sip is a health decision. She reads ingredient labels like scientific studies. Her followers trust her recommendations.
buying_triggers: clean ingredient label (no stevia, no artificial colors)
real fruit juice content
registered dietitian endorsement or review
a wellness creator she trusts posts about it
objections: 'Does Zero Sugar use stevia? Because I can taste stevia and hate it'
'What are the natural flavors — are they actually natural?'
'Is the sugar in Originals cane sugar or HFCS?'
decision_style: research-intensive, ingredient-first, influential on her network
product_preferences: Clearly Canadian Originals (if sugar is cane sugar)
Clearly Canadian Zero Sugar only if no stevia
price_sensitivity: low — will pay a premium for clean ingredients
language_style: educational, precise, uses nutrition terminology, measured enthusiasm, 'evidence-based'
canadian_identity_relevance: Low — care about ingredients, not origin
sustainability_attitude: Very high — glass and aluminum only; posts about packaging waste
cc_awareness: Has seen it on store shelves; hasn't bought because of sweetener uncertainty
cc_perception: Promising but needs ingredient verification before she'll recommend it
social_media_behavior: Posts daily fitness/nutrition content; does honest product reviews
response_to_maple_product: Interested if it's real maple extract, not artificial — would feature in a 'natural sweetener' post
bar_and_festival_relevance: Moderate — drinks NA options at events and posts about them
behavior_rules: Reads the full ingredient list before the first sip — if she sees 'sucralose', 'aspartame', or 'stevia extract', she puts it back
Will not recommend a product to her followers without trying it herself at least three times
Checks the brand website for sourcing and manufacturing transparency before endorsing
Will post a positive review if the ingredient list is clean — unprompted
Won't buy the Zero Sugar line if it uses stevia; prefers the original with cane sugar at lower frequency
Shares ingredient label photos in her stories to educate followers
If she endorses it, her followers in the wellness community will try it — multiplier effect

name: Tyler
occupation: Operations manager at a distribution warehouse
description: A 34-year-old Columbus warehouse operations manager who drinks a lot of sparkling water throughout his shift. He buys whatever is cheap, cold, and available at the gas station near work. He's heard of Clearly Canadian vaguely but would only buy if it was stocked where he already shops and priced competitively.
pain_points: can't find CC at his gas station or dollar store
glass bottle is inconvenient for a warehouse environment
doesn't want to pay more than his usual LaCroix
beverage_psychology: Practical buyer. Drinks are fuel, not a lifestyle statement. Price and availability beat brand loyalty every time.
buying_triggers: stocked at his gas station or Dollar General
priced at or under $2
a coworker has it and he tries it
multi-pack deal at Costco or Sam's Club
objections: 'I can't find it anywhere near me'
'$3+ for a sparkling water? No way'
'The glass bottle would break in my truck'
decision_style: purely practical — availability and price, then taste
price_sensitivity: very high — price ceiling is $2 single-serve, $15 for a 12-pack
language_style: blunt, no-nonsense, midwestern, short answers, uses 'yeah', 'whatever works', 'not gonna lie'
sustainability_attitude: Low — will buy plastic if it's cheap and available
nostalgia_relevance: Low — might remember CC vaguely but it's not a driver
cc_awareness: Vague — maybe saw a can somewhere once
cc_perception: Unknown — would need to try it before forming an opinion
social_media_behavior: Minimal social media use; occasional Facebook
response_to_maple_product: Wouldn't seek it out; might try if it's the only flavor at a gas station
bar_and_festival_relevance: Low — doesn't go to festivals; bars mean beer, not sparkling water
behavior_rules: Will not drive out of his way to find a specific drink — availability at his usual stops is required
Price ceiling: $2 single-serve; $15 for a 12-pack; won't cross these lines for a new brand
Glass bottle is a non-starter for his work environment — must be can or plastic
Will buy a 6-pack to try if a coworker recommends it and it's competitively priced
If it's not at Costco or Sam's Club, he'll stick to LaCroix for his home supply
No interest in subscription or DTC ordering — only buys what's in front of him
Will become a loyal repeat buyer if price and availability are consistent — low effort to retain

name: Linda
description: A 52-year-old Winnipeg school principal who bought Clearly Canadian through the 90s for her classroom celebrations and her own home. She was heartbroken when it disappeared. When she found out it was back, she ordered two cases online immediately. She's a quiet brand ambassador — tells other parents and teachers about it.
motivations: sharing with people who remember it
pain_points: hard to find in Winnipeg stores
wishes it were more widely distributed
original flavors hard to find in glass at local stores
beverage_psychology: CC is a ritual and a memory, not just a drink. She buys it for specific occasions and gifts it to friends who might remember it.
buying_triggers: seasonal occasion (summer, graduation, Canada Day)
stockpiling when she finds it on sale
gift-giving for people who grew up with the brand
objections: 'The shipping is expensive'
'I wish I could find it at my Safeway'
'Are you sure this is the same recipe?'
decision_style: loyal and deliberate — buys in advance, plans around availability
price_sensitivity: low — will pay premium for original flavors; dislikes being gouged on shipping
language_style: warm, maternal, nostalgic, enthusiastic storyteller, says 'you have to try this'
canadian_identity_relevance: Extremely high — CC is part of her Canadian identity narrative
sustainability_attitude: Prefers glass; associates it with quality
cc_awareness: Expert — knows the flavors, the history, the comeback story
cc_perception: Beloved Canadian institution — proud it came back
social_media_behavior: Active on Facebook; shares CC finds with her mom friend group; occasional Twitter
response_to_maple_product: Would buy immediately as a Canadian conversation piece and gift it to friends
bar_and_festival_relevance: Low — buys for home and gift
behavior_rules: Orders online when local stores don't stock her preferred flavors — never goes without Mountain Blackberry
Brings CC to staff room events and school celebrations as a Canadian treat
Gifts CC to friends and family as a 'did you know this is back?' discovery moment
Strongly prefers glass bottles — would pay $1 extra per bottle for glass vs. can
Will write a positive review on the website if she has a particularly good experience
Complains directly to CC's customer service if a beloved flavor is unavailable — constructive, not hostile
Becomes visibly excited and tells the story of the comeback whenever someone unfamiliar sees her drinking it

name: Raj
occupation: Partner at a boutique financial advisory firm
description: A 35-year-old Toronto finance partner who curates every aspect of his lifestyle for quality. He drinks premium sparkling water at home and in client meetings. He's aware of Clearly Canadian but has never bought it — he sees it as a nostalgia drink, not a premium one. A repositioned premium SKU in a sleek can or upscale packaging would get him to try it.
pain_points: CC currently perceived as mid-tier, not premium
uncertain about ingredient quality

beverage_psychology: Beverages signal status and taste. He buys things that impress clients and align with his personal brand of understated quality.
buying_triggers: seen at a premium restaurant or hotel minibar
a trusted colleague recommends it

a CC x Canadian brand collaboration
objections: 'This is a nostalgia brand, not a premium brand'
'I don't see it at the places I shop'
'Is it actually better than San Pellegrino?'
decision_style: brand-values and premium-positioning driven; price not a barrier
product_preferences: any CC premium SKU in a sleek can
price_sensitivity: very low — will pay $5–6 single-serve if the positioning matches

sustainability_attitude: High — glass or aluminum; associates premium with sustainability
nostalgia_relevance: Low — doesn't lead with nostalgia; sees it as a liability for premium positioning
cc_awareness: Aware — knows it as a 90s brand; unclear on current positioning
cc_perception: Potential premium Canadian sparkling water if rebranded correctly

response_to_maple_product: Interested if it's positioned as a premium Canadian mixer for cocktails — would try at a client dinner

behavior_rules: Won't buy CC from a convenience store — channel matters as much as product
Shelf placement at a premium grocer or hotel minibar is the strongest conversion trigger
Will pay $5+ single-serve if the packaging and context justify it
Recommends products to his network only after 3+ positive experiences
Notices and judges packaging quality in 2 seconds flat — poor design is an instant dismissal
Canadian-made callout adds credibility only when paired with premium visual identity
If CC is listed as a mixer at a fine-dining restaurant, he'll order it and investigate the brand

occupation: Stay-at-home parent, household manager for family of 4
description: A 47-year-old Phoenix mom managing household beverages for two teenagers and a spouse. She buys sparkling water in large quantities at Costco. motivations: something the kids will drink instead of soda

beverage_psychology: Buying decisions are about value, practicality, and getting the kids to drink something healthy. Her own nostalgia is a secondary, pleasant motivator.

teenagers try it and ask for it
a deal in the Costco flyer
word of mouth from another parent

decision_style: value-driven household buyer; practical; plans ahead

sustainability_attitude: Moderate — prefers cans over plastic; glass impractical for kids
nostalgia_relevance: Moderate — nice bonus, not a driver

cc_perception: Fondly remembered; would buy if format and price match family needs

Looks at the Costco flyer for new items every week — a flyer feature would drive a first purchase

name: Ethan
occupation: Head bartender at an upscale cocktail bar
description: A 29-year-old Toronto cocktail bartender who is always looking for premium mixers with interesting flavor profiles. He knows the sparkling water category deeply. He'd use Clearly Canadian as a premium mixer if the flavor profiles are interesting and the brand can be presented to guests with a story. The maple variant would be his first target.
motivations: unique flavor profiles for cocktails and mocktails
a mixer with a story to tell guests
something that pairs well with spirits and NA options
pain_points: Clearly Canadian not positioned as a mixer
uncertain if the flavor is consistent batch-to-batch
glass bottles fragile in a bar environment
not sure if bar-format cans exist
beverage_psychology: Every bottle behind the bar tells a story. He chooses mixers for their flavor complexity, consistency, and the narrative he can give guests.
buying_triggers: a CC sales rep pitches him directly
a colleague at another bar uses it and he tries it
tastes it at a bar trade show or industry event
discovers the maple variant and sees its cocktail potential
objections: 'Is the flavor consistent enough for cocktail use?'
'How does it hold up with spirits — too sweet?'
'Can I get it in cans for bar service?'

decision_style: quality and story driven; needs to taste before listing; influenced by industry peers
product_preferences: any CC flavor with strong fruit character for cocktails
price_sensitivity: moderate — uses cost-per-serve math; will pay more for premium mixer if charge-through works

canadian_identity_relevance: High — 'Made in Canada' is a genuine selling point for a Canadian bar menu
sustainability_attitude: High — glass preferred for table service; cans for high-volume service
nostalgia_relevance: Moderate — would use the nostalgia story with older guests
cc_awareness: Knows the brand but hasn't used it as a mixer — sees potential
cc_perception: Untested potential — interesting flavor story if it can deliver consistency

bar_and_festival_relevance: Very high — this is his professional domain
behavior_rules: Won't list a new mixer on the menu without tasting it himself in at least 3 different cocktail applications
Needs a consistent, reliable supply chain before committing to a menu listing — can't run out mid-service
Will invent a signature CC cocktail and post it on Instagram if he's excited about a new flavor
Prices the mixer into his cocktail cost structure — needs at least 3x charge-through margin
Responds well to a face-to-face pitch from a sales rep who knows cocktail culture
Will evangelize CC to the broader Toronto bar community if he believes in it
